finalized.subtitle: Real talk + escalating personal energy
finalized.hook_text: Here's why "fee waived" on your wire receipt doesn't mean the wire was free.
finalized.final_vo: Here's why "fee waived" on your wire receipt doesn't mean the wire was free. Your bank isn't really your friend on cross-border wires. They send you a wire confirmation that says fee waived. You think they're doing you a favour. They're often not. Here's why. The bank may have already added a 3 percent markup into the FX rate they used. FX means foreign exchange, the rate that turns one currency into another. That 3 percent more than covers the wire fee they waived. On a 10,000 dollar wire, that 3 percent markup is around 300 dollars. You call the bank. They show you the receipt. Fee waived. They're not lying. The money came out of the rate, not the fee. They never have to call it a fee. Every wire. Same pattern. This happens to business owners every week. We built WorldFirst to skip that markup. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows when you search USD to CNY. Same supplier. Same 10,000 dollar wire. The 300 dollars stays in your business. Search WorldFirst.

finalized.subtitle: Cold open + compounding math
finalized.hook_text: Here's what every business owner needs to know about how wire rate margin compounds across a year.
finalized.final_vo: Here's what every business owner needs to know about how wire rate margin compounds across a year. Three business owners sent 50K to the same Chinese supplier on the same day. Supplier received three different amounts. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. A 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Now compound it. 12 wires a year at 50K each. 600 thousand dollars in sourcing volume. The business owner who used the bank lost around 9,000 dollars. The business owner who used the specialist lost around 1,800. That's a 7,200 dollar gap in one year. On the same business. Run it across three years. 21,600 dollars. That's an employee. That's a container of inventory. That's the next product line. And it's not a fee. It's the exchange rate margin. Banks bake 1 to 2% into the rate so you never see the line item. WorldFirst sits closer to 0.3% on the same wires. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Search WorldFirst.

finalized.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
finalized.hook_text: Here's what every business owner needs to know about the math of paying suppliers in USD.
finalized.final_vo: Here's what every business owner needs to know about the math of paying suppliers in USD. Here's the math on a 20,000 dollar payment. Your bank converts your local currency to USD. That's a 1% margin. 200 dollars gone. Your supplier's Chinese bank then converts that USD to CNY. Another 0.5%. 100 dollars gone. SWIFT and intermediary fees, another 60 dollars. Total leakage, 360 dollars. Plus 4 days of waiting. Now pay the same 20K in CNY directly. One conversion at 0.4%. That's 80 dollars total. Settles same day. The difference is 280 dollars on one order. Run 12 orders a year and that's 3,360 dollars. This is not a fee problem. This is a routing problem. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers same-day. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

finalized.subtitle: Cold open + compounding stakes
finalized.hook_text: Is it possible to lose a container in five days without losing the money?
finalized.final_vo: Is it possible to lose a container in five days without losing the money? Your bank app shows sent. Your supplier's WhatsApp shows nothing. You haven't lost the money. You've lost the day. Then the week. Then the order. Because your supplier won't load raw materials until the CNY hits their account. So every hour the wire sits at an intermediary bank, your delivery date slips. Three intermediaries, two days of conversion windows, one AML hold above a threshold nobody told you about, plus a Friday public holiday in Shenzhen. That's a five-day silence. On a Q4 order, five days is your container. On a launch SKU, five days is your competitor beating you to the shelf. The wire isn't lost. The visibility is. WorldFirst removes the correspondent chain entirely. CNY straight to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, with a tracking reference your supplier can match. Search WorldFirst.

finalized.subtitle: Real talk + supplier's view
finalized.hook_text: Why your supplier hates when you pay them by credit card.
finalized.final_vo: Why your supplier hates when you pay them by credit card. Picture both sides of the table. You look at the credit card and see a way to pay. You see a 3 percent fee, and you don't mind eating it. Your supplier sees that same 3 percent coming out of their margin. The merchant pays the card fees, not the cardholder. You see chargeback protection. Your supplier sees 180 days where the bank can reverse the payment, on goods they already shipped. You see Visa as a familiar tool. Your supplier sees a system 95 percent of their factory peers do not even use. The card is not bad. It is just not built for this kind of B2B trade. This happens every week. Every single week. WorldFirst pays suppliers in their local currency on Alipay and direct bank rails. The systems they actually use. Search WorldFirst.

finalized.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
finalized.hook_text: Here's what every business owner needs to know about the Google FX rate.
finalized.final_vo: Here's what every business owner needs to know about the Google FX rate. Type THB to CNY. Whatever number it shows you, that rate does not exist for your wallet. That's the mid-market rate. It's the rate banks use among themselves. The rate you actually get is a different number, a worse number, and the difference is called the spread. On a personal tool, that spread is usually 2 to 4 percent. On a business platform, it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same day. Same minute. Two different rates. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire, 2 percent is 400 dollars. 0.5 percent is 100 dollars. That's 300 dollars that disappeared inside the rate. No line item. No fee notification. Just a worse number than Google showed you. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates direct to business owners. Search WorldFirst.

finalized.subtitle: Real talk + the WhatsApp scenario
finalized.hook_text: For every business owner who's been told no to Visa by a Chinese supplier.
finalized.final_vo: For every business owner who's been told no to Visa by a Chinese supplier. Here's the real reason, and it has nothing to do with technology. Your supplier sells you a product at a thin margin. Say 8 percent. When you pay by Visa, they pay 3 percent in card fees on their side. That cuts their 8 percent margin to 5. Then there's the 180-day chargeback window where you can reverse the payment on goods already shipped. So they have to hold cash they cannot fully count as theirs for six months. Now multiply that by every Visa order. Most factories in China run thin. They cannot absorb cards. That's not stubbornness. That's math. WorldFirst pays in the local currency, direct to their bank. Same supplier, no card fees, no chargeback hold. They ship faster. You pay less. Search WorldFirst.

finalized.subtitle: Real talk + frustration escalation
finalized.hook_text: Here is what every growing importer needs to know about why scale feeds the bank.
finalized.final_vo: Here is what every growing importer needs to know about why scale feeds the bank. They're working harder every year and somehow keeping less. Sound familiar? Picture this. The owner added two new product lines. They doubled the marketing budget. They even found a better factory in Guangzhou. And the gross margin still dropped. Here's what nobody told them. The bank takes 4% of the sourcing budget every year through the exchange rate margin, the hidden gap inside the swap from ringgit or USD into Chinese yuan. The bigger the business grows, the bigger the bank's cut. Walk through it. On 500,000 dollars of sourcing, the bank takes 20,000 dollars a year. Scale up to one million dollars in sourcing, the bank takes 40,000 dollars. Double the work, double their take. The business is effectively subsidising the bank's growth with its own. Meanwhile, the owner is losing sleep over a 50 cent difference on a unit price. The leak is not the factory. The leak is the payment route. WorldFirst, the international payment service, sits at 0.3 to 0.5% on the same kind of payments. The 4% stops bleeding. The scale starts compounding for the business, not for the bank. finalized.subtitle: 30-second timer + brutal specificity
finalized.hook_text: Can a business owner find a hidden foreign exchange fee in 30 seconds? Watch.
finalized.final_vo: Can a business owner find a hidden foreign exchange fee in 30 seconds? Watch. Watch. Step one. Pull up your last wire confirmation. Five seconds. Find the field that says applied rate or exchange rate used. Got it. Step two. Open a new tab. Google USD to the local currency you wired to. Look at the rate. That's the mid-market rate. Five seconds. Step three. Compare. Your bank's rate versus Google's rate. The gap is the margin. Twenty seconds in, you've found it. Step four. Multiply the gap by the wire amount. Ten seconds. That's the fee. No line item. No receipt. Just hidden inside the rate. Most business owners wire 50K to 100K per order. A 2 percent gap on a 50K wire is 1,000 dollars. Per wire. WorldFirst gives you a rate near Google's mid-market. The hidden fee disappears. Search WorldFirst.
